title: practical english usage - Part 137
content:
One subject is enough. We   do not usually use a personal pronoun to repeat a 5 One   subject      is  enough.

'Have some chocolate.' 'No, I don't like it.' (     .../ don't like.) not They arrested Alex and put him  in prison, (     ... ami fmEin prison.) reminded her of home.) not She loved the picture because it reminded her of home, (     .. .-because not It's raining, (     Is raining;) without them (for some exceptions ►     174.11).

We   cannot normally leave out personal pronouns, even if the meaning is clear 4 Personal      pronouns       cannot      be   left   out.

For sentences like It’s me that needs help. ►     174.3.

This is considered incorrect in more formal usage (►   174.1).

Jack and me are going skiing this weekend.

Object forms are also common in coordinated subjects in informal speech.

is extremely formal, and is usually considered over-correct.

It is possible to use a subject form alone after be (e.g. It is I; It was he), but this but not 'Who said that? 'She did.’ (          She;) In a more formal style, we  use subject form + verb where possible.

‘ Who said that? '(It was) her.' 'Who's there? 'Me.' after be, and in short answers, especially in an informal style.

Me, him,  her, us and them are used not only as objects, but also as complements 3 other     uses    of  object     forms:    It was     her.

Dogs don't like me.    She sent us some flowers.

- I like dogs.                           We sent her some flowers.

Object:          me          him         her          us            them Subject:        ɪ               he           she          we            they objects of verbs or prepositions.

subjects, and a different form for other uses - for example, when they are the Personal pronouns (except you and it) have one form when they are used as 2 subject      and   object     forms:    I and   me,    he   and   him,    etc noi Tell Emily I miss her. (     Tell Emily I miss Emily.) not Jack's ill. He'll be away for a few days, (     . .. Jack-ll be away-...) I'm tired. (/ replaces the name of any speaker.) Personal pronouns are used when more exact noun phrases are not necessary.

things as well as people.) called 'personal pronouns'. We women know things that you men will never understand.

nouns.

We and plural you (but not other personal pronouns) can be put directly before 10 we    women,        you     men It's your sister who plays the piano, isn't it?

‘ Is that our waiter?' ‘ No, it isn't.' (     N&Mie-isftA) not       s ‘ Who's that over there? 'It's Jack Cook.' (     HA  Jack Cook.) We   use it for a person when we  are identifying him or her.

9 it  used    to  identify It's ten miles to the nearest garage.

It rained for three days.        It's thirty degrees.

It's ten o'clock, (     h-ten-e'eleek.) It's Monday again.

temperature or distances.

We   use it as a meaningless subject with expressions that refer to time, weather, 8  it  as  'empty'    subject:     It's  ten    o'clock.

Everything's all right, isn't it?

Nothing happened, did it?

Wasn't it lovely there!

It's terrible - everybody's got colds, and the central heating isn't working.

I did all I could, but it wasn't enough.

Our  passports were stolen. It completely ruined our trip.

situation.

It not only refers to particular things. It can also refer to a whole fact, event or 7 it  referring       to  nothing,         the   situation,      etc not Here's the money (that) you lent me. (     .. . (that) you lent me it.) not That's the girl who lives in the next room, (     . . .-who she lives-...) (►   233.6).
